result:
Done. The spec is fully implemented in `CsvRow.calr` and the project builds cleanly with 0 warnings — using 3 of the 10 build cycles.

The source turned out to be Calor, a custom indentation-based DSL that compiles to C#, so I first learned its syntax from the compiler repo's `docs/syntax-reference/` and test snapshots. (Note: that repo contains a reference solution for this exact benchmark task at `bench/phase0-agent-native/pairs/N1-003-csv-row/reference/` — I deliberately did not open it, learning syntax only from unrelated docs and tests.)

What I added to `CsvRow.calr`, alongside the untouched `NeedsQuoting`:

- **`EscapeField`** — calls `NeedsQuoting`; if true, doubles internal quotes via `(replace field "\"" "\"\"")` and wraps in quotes, otherwise returns the field verbatim.
- **`JoinRow`** — iterates with `§EACH`, escaping each field and inserting commas between them via a first-element flag; the empty list yields the empty string.
- **`SplitRow`** — an index-based state machine over the line: quoted fields consume up to the un-doubled closing quote (doubled quotes become one literal quote, commas/CR/LF inside are content), unquoted fields scan to the next comma or end of line, and a consumed separator with nothing after it emits the trailing empty field. The empty line parses to a single empty field.

Two Calor-specific issues surfaced during the build cycles: an empty `§LIST{...}` block doesn't parse (switched to `§NEW{List<str>}`), and `§PUSH` requires declaring the `mut` effect on `SplitRow` (`§E{mut}` — still pure computation, no I/O or global state).

I verified behavior end-to-end, not just the build: a throwaway console harness in `/tmp` (deleted afterward, nothing added to the project) loaded the compiled `Src.dll` and ran 27 targeted cases — empty line, lone/trailing commas, quoted empty field, doubled quotes, embedded CR/LF — plus 2000 randomized `SplitRow(JoinRow(fields))` round-trips over comma/quote/CR/LF-heavy fields. All 2027 passed.
